test_description: This test case evaluates the generator model's accuracy and technical depth, assuming a perfect retrieval process.
question: [Judge] The introduction of leveling bars with widths approximately one-fifth of the minimum feature dimension and placed at distances about equal to the minimum feature dimension from isolated edges on a photolithographic mask effectively reduces proximity effects by altering the edge intensity gradient, thereby causing the final resist line width of isolated lines to more closely match that of densely packed lines. (True/False)

gold_context_sources.section_title: US5242770: Delta=Absolute Value of ((line width 39)— (line width 37 or 41))
gold_context_sources.evidence: The information shown in Table 6C shows that intensity leveled line 41 has line width values much closer to the line width values of densely packed line 39 when compared to unleveled line width values. In other words, by utilizing edge intensity leveling bars the final resist line width of an isolated line will closely match the final line width of a densely packed line. Whereas, the final resist line width of an isolated line without edge intensity leveling will not match. The leveling bars are not imageable under pattern transfer conditions and are completely developed away when a nominal exposure energy (En) is applied. The nominal exposure energy is 150 mJoules. The leveling bars have widths approximately one-fifth of the minimum dimension and are placed at distances approximately equal to the minimum dimension or 1.1 times the minimum dimension from isolated edges.

ground_truth_answer: The correct answer is True. Reasoning: According to the detailed description in US5242770, introducing leveling bars of narrow width (approximately one-fifth of the minimum feature dimension) placed at a distance roughly equal to the minimum feature dimension from isolated edges modifies the edge intensity gradient. This modification reduces proximity effects by causing the resist line width of isolated lines to more closely match that of densely packed lines. The leveling bars are designed to be non-imageable (i.e., they do not print on the substrate) under nominal exposure conditions, ensuring no additional lines appear in the pattern. This approach effectively improves pattern fidelity and reduces critical dimension variation due to proximity effects, as explicitly supported by the evidence.
